perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any Company proprietary information, technical data, trade secrets or know -how, including, but not limited to, research, product plans, products, services, customer lists, markets, publicly available data, software, developments, inventions, processes, formulas, technology, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, finances or other business information disclosed to the Contractor by the Company either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.explanation: The modification includes 'publicly available data' within the definition of 'Confidential Information'. This contradicts the common understanding and legal definition of trade secrets, which must be kept secret to maintain their protected status.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. Including 'publicly available data' directly contradicts this requirement of secrecy and independent economic value derived from that secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text includes 'publicly available data' within the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This directly contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, which requires secrecy to maintain its protected status. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that UTSA defines a trade secret as deriving economic value from not being generally known, which 'publicly available data' inherently is. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 404 error, the Law Explanation provides a sufficient legal basis for determining that including publicly available data in the definition of confidential information creates a contradiction, based on the explanation given.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of the Term, the Contractor will return to the Company, retaining nocopies or notes, all documents relating to the Company’s business including, but not limit ed to,reports, abstracts, lists, correspondence, information, computer files, computer disks, and allother materials and all copies of such material, obtained by the Contractor during itscontractual relationship with the company.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of the Term, the Contractor will return to the Company, retaining no copies or notes, all documents relating to the Company’s business including, but not limited to, reports, abstracts, lists, correspondence, information, computer files, computer disks, and all other materials and all copies of such material, except for those materials necessary for the Contractor to demonstrate compliance with applicable laws and regulations, obtained by the Contractor during its contractual relationship with the company.
perturbation.explanation: This clause mandates the return of all documents, but the modified text introduces an exception for materials needed for legal compliance. While seemingly reasonable, it contradicts legal requirements in certain industries (e.g., finance, healthcare) where contractors *must* retain certain records for a specified period to demonstrate compliance with regulations such as HIPAA or Sarbanes-Oxley. The 'return all' clause without considering legal retention requirements creates a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act (specifically 18 U.S. Code § 1519) makes it a crime to knowingly alter, destroy, mutilate, conceal, cover up, falsify, or make a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case. By compelling return of all documents, even those required to be retained for compliance and potential audits, the original text conflicts with the spirit and practical application of SOX.

Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matt
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates the return of *all* documents, which the LLM explanation argues could conflict with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) and other regulations that require certain records to be retained for compliance purposes. The modified text introduces an exception, which seemingly resolves this issue but simultaneously highlights the original clause's potential conflict. The provided snippet from 18 USC 1519 prohibits the destruction or alteration of records with the intent to obstruct investigations. While the original text doesn't explicitly instruct the contractor to destroy documents, compelling the return of all documents, including those legally required to be retained, could lead to a situation where the contractor is unable to comply with record retention laws, thus creating a potential violation. The 'return all' clause, especially in industries with strict retention mandates, creates a direct conflict by potentially forcing actions that contradict legal compliance obligations, thus a contradiction exists based on misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information is provided “AS IS” and the Company makes no warrantyregarding the accuracy or reliability of such information. Neither the Company nor any of itsadvisors or representatives shall have any liability to the Contractor or any of its representatives resulting from the Contractor ’s use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is provided “AS IS” and the Company makes no warranty regarding the accuracy or reliability of such information, except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct. Neither the Company nor any of its advisors or representatives shall have any liability to the Contractor or any of its representatives resulting from the Contractor’s use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct' introduces a contradiction. While it seems to provide a standard limitation of liability, it can be interpreted to negate the 'AS IS' provision entirely, especially if the definition of Confidential Information is broad. The implication that the company has *some* responsibility for the information's accuracy when gross negligence is involved clashes with the disclaimer of warranties.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 outlines the requirements for disclaiming warranties. While an 'as is' clause can effectively disclaim implied warranties, adding exceptions for gross negligence or willful misconduct creates ambiguity, potentially undermining the effectiveness of the disclaimer. Courts may interpret this exception as implying a limited warranty, thus contradicting the original intent of providing the information 'as is' without any warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an exception ('except in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct') to the 'AS IS' clause, creating a legal contradiction. UCC § 2-316, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows for disclaimers like 'as is' to exclude warranties. However, the addition of the exception introduces ambiguity. The exception undermines the original intent of providing the information 'as is' without warranty. This is because it implies a limited warranty in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, which clashes with the initial disclaimer. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this point: The company may have some responsibility for the information if gross negligence is involved, contradicting the 'AS IS' clause.

perturbation.original_text: The parties are independent of each other. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed to createan employment or joint relationship between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties are independent of each other, unless the Company directs the Contractor in detail on how to perform specific tasks. Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed to create an employment or joint relationship between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause, 'unless the Company directs the Contractor in detail on how to perform specific tasks,' can legally imply an employer-employee relationship despite the explicit statement to the contrary.  Behavioral control is a key factor used to determine worker classification.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 3121(d)(2) defines an employee, in part, as someone who has the status of an employee under the usual common law rules applicable in determining the employer-employee relationship.  The IRS and courts consider behavioral control (the extent to which the business controls how the worker does their job) a key factor. The modification explicitly states that the company may control how the contractor performs tasks, contradicting the 'independent contractor' status asserted in the original text.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: endent contractors Gig workers Small business Large business Charities and nonprofits International taxpayers Governmental liaisons Federal, state and local governments Indian tribal governments Tax exempt bonds Taxpayer identification numbers (TIN) People such as doctors, dentists, veterinarians, lawyers, accountants, contractors, subcontractors, public stenographers, or auctioneers who are in an independent trade, business, or profession in which they offer their services to the general public are generally independent contractors. However, whether these people are independent contractors or employees depends on the facts in each case. The general rule is that an individual is an independent contractor if the person for whom the services are performed has the right to control or direct only the result of the work and not what will be done and how it will be done. If you are an independent contractor, then you are self-employed. The earnings of a person who is working as an independent contractor are subject to self-employment tax . To find out what your tax obligations are, visit the Self-Employed Individuals Tax Center . Generally, the person for whom the services are performed must report payments to independent contractors on Form 1099-NEC, Nonemployee Compensation , in box 1. You are not an independent contractor if you perform services that can be controlled by an employer (what will be done and how it will be done). This applies even if you are given freedom of action. What matters is that the employer has the legal right to control the details of how the services are performed. If an employer-employee relationship exists (regardless of what the relationship is called), then you are not an independent contractor and your earnings are generally not subject to self-employment tax. However, your earnings as an employee may be subject to FICA (social security and Medicare tax) and income tax withholding. Generally, earnings of an employee are reported on Form W-2, Wage and Tax Statement . Refer to the page, Independent Contractor (Self-Employed) or Employee? or Publication 15-A, Employer’s Supplement Tax Guide , for more information. Related Topics Reporting payments to independent contractor Estimated taxes Backup withholding Topic no. 307, Backup withholding Page Last Reviewed or Updated: 02-Jun-2025 Share Facebook Twitter Linkedin Print
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition where the company can direct the contractor in detail, which contradicts the independent contractor status. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "You are not an independent contractor if you perform services that can be controlled by an employer (what will be done and how it will be done)." The modification creates a legal contradiction because it allows for a level of control that legally implies an employer-employee relationship, undermining the initial declaration of an independent relationship. This is a direct contradiction as the altered clause permits the company to dictate how tasks are performed, a characteristic of an employer-employee relationship, thus failing the legal test for an independent contractor as defined in the snippet.
